title: The Macro Minute: Is the Trump administration successfully renegotiating global trade?

Happy Monday out there, team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Monday,
July 28th, 2025. Hope everyone had a
wonderful weekend. So, as always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. If you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following KISS and Dr. Mo need to
be a client of 42 macro. So today's
Kumacro question is, is the Trump
administration successfully
renegotiating global trade? Our answer,
the US and EU agreed to a trade deal
imposing 15% tariffs on most EU exports.
Although like Japan, the two sides
appear to be in disagreement on key
details. The US EU trade pack levies 15%
tariffs on most EU exports, including
automobiles, diffusing a potential trade
war that could have weighed
substantially upon the global economy.
Senior US officials confirmed the 15%
tariff applies to EU pharmaceutical and
semiconductor exports pending in section
232 probe due in three weeks. The 15%
tariff on pharmaceuticals and
semiconductors was contingent on the
EU's promise to boost US investment.
Specifically, the EU agreed to purchase
750 billion in US energy products and
invest 600 billion in the US, 50 billion
more than the investment pledge agreed
to by Japan last week. The EU and Japan
uh trade deals represent clear evidence
of the Trump administration successfully
using the threat of section 232 tariffs
as negotiating leverage to secure better
terms on the bilateral front. For our
April 10th leadoff morning note,
yesterday the S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100
melted up 9.5% and 12% respectively,
their best day since October 2008 after
President Trump postponed the greater
than 10% portion of reciprocal tariffs
by 90 days. Part of the reason markets
rallied so hard yesterday that was that
this was the first time anyone thought
the art of the deal could actually work
to realign global trade with relatively
minimal disruption, ourselves included.
Elsewhere, US and Chinese officials are
meeting in Stockholm for their third
round of trade talks in three months led
by Chinese Vice Premier Leah Fang and
you US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessie.
Talks in Stockholm will cover the future
of the USChina trade tariff troops,
fentanyl linked to US tariffs, and
Chinese imports of sanctioned Russian
and Iranian oil. The wide scope suggests
plenty of room for disappointment.
Additionally, recent trade deals with
the Japan and EU suggests the US may
negotiate harder than it otherwise would
have. The latest US China bilateral
trade talks come amid news that China
will begin national child care subsidies
to counter plunging birth rates
threatening its labor force and
productivity. This is consistent with
our long-held view that Beijing would
pivot to fiscal policy to support the
Chinese economy because it it too is
committed to a strategic decoupling of
the US and Chinese economies. Lastly,
this week's unrelenting barrage of key
macro and micro data threatens to
justify our crowding models recent
short-term bear signal for the S&P 500.
As always, Team42 can trust us to to ext
effectively extract signals from all the
noise. So on Monday we have obviously
the US and China uh third round of trade
talks. Tuesday's highlight includes uh
the June Jones report. Wednesday's
highlights include the Q2 GDP from the
US uh the Q3 quarterly refunding
announcement uh and the FOMC uh
decision. We also have Meta and
Microsoft earnings as well as Q2 uh GDP
from the Euro zone and the Bank of
Canada's rate decision. On Thursday we
have uh the PC report from the US, the

index from the US. Jabba's claims,

as well. And then we also have the Bank

kiss and Dr. Mo will keep our clients on
the right side of market risk
throughout. Um transitioning to our 42
micro dashboard as always wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
titled uh the equity market and the
10-year Treasury yield. Says, "Hi
Darius, thanks for your analysis in this
week's round the horn publication. I'm
learning a great deal from your in-depth
and thoughtful analysis and in
particular the market and societal
implications of the foretturning. You
indicated that due to the US's acute
fiscal problem, uh, US and global equity
markets tend to malfunction, um, if the
10-year Treasury reaches 4.75 to 5%.
Also, you are of the view that the Fed
should lower the Fed funds rate given
the slowing growth in private sector
labor incomes and to help the Treasury
fund the fiscal deficit by issuing bills
at lower interest rates. The lowering of
the Fed funds rate will likely uh not
necessarily cause the 10-year Treasury
to rise as the Treasury will shift the
issuance of Treasury securities to bills
and will limit and possibly eliminate in
the near term the issuance of notes and
bonds. This will negate the supply
demand imbalance in the Treasury bond
market at least for the short term.
However, I have a concern that when the
Fed lowers and the Fed funds uh when the
Fed lowers the Fed funds rate, the
market may price in rising inflation
expectations given paradigm C and a
booming economy and the 10-year yield
will rise despite the lack of issuance
at the long end. If this plays out, Kiss
and Dr. Mo will identify will kiss and
Dr. Mo identify the rising downside risk
in the US equity market as the 10-year
Treasury yield starts to rise. And as an
aside, anyone reading this post may find
the views of Jim Carson informative as
expressed in his recent interview on the
top traders unplugged podcast. This
whole interview is worth listening to,
but uh the most relevant part of this
discussion is from minute 52 to 56. He
reads that the only way out of the US's
fiscal situation is for the Fed to
eventually monetize the debt. So um
great question, obviously a long
question, a lot to unpack. Uh first just
want to say thank you to the um to the

on Friday and I think it's worth

general expectation I should you as an
investor should be that when the Fed
lowers the interest rate policy rate
bond yields also are likely to decline.
Um, you know, we talked about this at
Nauseium on Fridays, I think on Thursday
and Friday of last week. So, if you
missed those, uh, um, you know, macro
minutes, uh, definitely go back and
review those. I don't want to, um, you
know, rehash what we talked about then.
But the key takeaway is that the
assumption that Fed lowering the policy
rate all of a sudden is now, you know,
bullish for the bond yield, bearish for
bond prices is is, in my opinion, you're
you're you're anchoring on the recency
bias of last fall. That's not
traditionally what happens.
Traditionally what happened is that
there's a role as interest rates fall
down the curve a roll um down the curve
and there tends to be some decline. Now
clearly if the market if the market
thought that any rate cuts were being
sort of pushed through for political
reasons which is highly unlikely given
chairman pow's um you know resistance uh
to to political pressure. Uh if if the
market thought these were politicized
rate cuts then very clearly we could see
some widening of term premia and
increase in inflation expectations on
the long game of the treasury curve.
That's not a guarantee, but it's it's
highly likely to occur if the market
deemed they were politicized as we
talked about last week and reiterating
again now the the fact that we saw a
sharp deceleration of private sector
labor income in the most recent jobs
report to 1.4% a well below trend level
of 1.4% which is the lowest rate
three-month annualized rate of growth
since uh July uh since June of 2020 and
then I think we slowed like three or 400
basis points sequentially. That was the
sharpest deceleration we've seen since
May of 2022. I mean, you know, there's a
very clearly, you know, in our opinion,
reasons for the Fed to uh, you know,
accept, you know, some, you know, easing
at the low end of the policy rate. And
quite frankly, the only reason they have
not is because they're concerned about,
uh, how tariffs, which their previous
research has always indicated was a
one-off price level adjustment, is
likely to impact inflation via the
expectations channel. Because by the
way, Chairpo and his colleagues at the
Fed were the pro were the reason that
the the the inflation genie got let out
of the bottle previously. So they're
kind of scared of their own shadow in
terms of uh in terms of changing uh
their views in terms of how tariffs are
likely to impact uh um inflation over
the long term. And in my opinion, I
think that's a a policy make on top of a
policy make in our view. Um so getting
back into the the actual ultimate
question is uh if this plays out, will
Kiss and Dr. Mo identify the rising
downside risk to the equity market as
the tenolio starts to rise? So again, if
this indy starts to rise just on any
rate cuts at the same time where
obviously a mired in a U-shaped economy,
that is not a good setup for the for the
markets. You're talking about growth
slowing with bond yields rising. That's
just that that's a terrible setup for
the market. So obviously you that that
increases the probability that we saw a
correction uh in the in the market. And
we define correction as a decline of 10%
or more in the S&P 500. that that
increased probability correction if the
probability was actually rising to you
know greater than 50% chance then it's
likely that you would see uh the risk
management overlays that we feature in

risk because ultimately the risk is is

scenario which I'm not saying is the

could easily morph into something worse
uh just given um you know just given um
you know just given the fact that you

valuation concerns in the markets. Now
again our highest probability path
forward is that markets generally remain
in risk on condition. The market regime
generally remains in a risk-on condition
because there still there's still a
cohort of investors that have yet to you
know fully recognize and ultimately
position for paradigm C which itself may
persist for multiple years if not three
or four years in the context of what
could potentially happen next fall in
the midterm election. So uh in our view

fall. Um, you know, you know, associated

making here is that you don't need to
live in fear of all these different
scenarios and tail risks. All you need
to do is dispassionately execute the
signals. And if the signals tell you to
start taking down risk, then you know
that the area the integrant under the
left side of the distribution called
left tail risk is rising in quantity and
rising and broadening out in scope and
that ultimately there might be some left
tail risk that needs to be priced in
